old_title: Ukraine war live: Putin launches 'biggest ballistic missile attack on Kyiv'
new_title: Ukraine war live: Moscow under attack as 400 Ukrainian drones hit Russian capital
old_text:
Ukrainian forces have struck three oil depots and a fuel facility in Russia's southern Stavropol region this morning, President Volodymyr Zelensky has said.
The Ukrainian president said three Russian "shadow fleet" tankers were also hit in the Black Sea.
It comes after a major Russian aerial assault on Kyiv and other cities this morning, which Ukrainian authorities said killed at least six people and injured 16 others.
The attack on Ukraine's capital is believed to be the biggest ballistic missile attack of the war.
The Kyiv government said firefighters were responding to blazes in five different districts after the attack, one of the biggest in recent weeks, hit residential buildings, office and industrial sites, a dormitory and vehicles.
Ukrainian foreign minister Andrii Sybiha said the raid involved "around four dozen" ballistic missiles, the largest number in a single attack since the war began.
The attack on Kyiv comes a day after Ukrainian drone strikes killed at least nine people at warehouses belonging to a major Russian shopping website.
Volodymyr Zelensky said the strikes targeted "significant logistical facilities", accusing Wildberries of providing equipment for drones and navigation equipment for the Russian military.
How Storm Shadow missiles could be replaced by new UK strike weapons
Britain has successfully tested new long-range strike weapons, with the government aiming to deliver them to Ukraine within months to bolster its defence capabilities.
The initiative, known as "Project Brakestop," seeks to produce munitions more quickly and affordably than current systems like Storm Shadow missiles, with specifications including a 500km range, 225kg warhead, and speeds over 600km per hour.
Initial trials took place at a range in the Hebrides, involving three remaining suppliers - MBDA UK, MGI Engineering, and Rotron Aerospace - after a competitive development process that saw 27 industry bids.
You can read more below:
Russian strike on cargo ship in Black Sea kills five, Kyiv says
Russian forces struck an international cargo vessel in the Black Sea off the coast of Ukraine's southern city of Odesa, killing at least five crew members, Ukrainian officials said on Sunday.
The Guinea-Bissau-flagged ship had left port loaded with corn and was carrying a crew of 17 sailors from India and Syria, in addition to one Ukrainian, said Prime Minister Sergii Koretskyi.
Eight crew members were rescued from the vessel - which was still burning late on Sunday - and five remain missing, he added.
A Reuters witness in Odesa saw smoke billowing from a large vessel off the coast. Moscow has not publicly commented on Sunday's strike. Russia has stepped up attacks in recent weeks on deepwater ports in southern Ukraine that handle much of the country's grain and other critical cargo in the fifth year of war.
"A civilian ship. An international crew. A route serving global food security. Putin saw another target," acting Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha wrote on X, referring to Russian President Vladimir Putin.
"This attack leaves no room for illusions. Putin's Russia does not care whom it kills."
On Saturday, one person was killed when Russia attacked port infrastructure in Odesa, hitting a vessel under the flag of Antigua and Barbuda. A day earlier, three people were killed in Russian strikes on Odesa and Mykolaiv.
Protesters rally in Kyiv as Zelensky moves to oust Ukraine's defense minister
Protesters gathered in downtown Kyiv earlier this week after Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky moved to dismiss his defence minister, as parliament convened to appoint a new prime minister as part of a major government reshuffle.
The shake-up could become a test of Zelensky's political authority as Ukraine's fight against Russia's full-scale invasion approaches four and a half years.
Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov, 35, is considered to be a moderniser whose technological expertise is credited in part with a significant improvement in Ukraine's military performance in recent months against Russia's bigger army. He was expected to leave the government after just six months in the post.
You can read more about the protests below:
Recap: Russia pounds Kyiv with 41 different types of missiles in overnight attack
Russia hit Kyiv with an overnight onslaught involving 41 different types of missiles in the early hours of Sunday morning, Volodymyr Zelensky said.
Residential buildings, warehouses, a supermarket and a dormitory were among the structures damaged, said Kyiv Mayor Vitali Klitschko, adding that three people were in a serious condition.
At one location in western Kyiv, emergency workers picked through smouldering debris and doused bombed-out apartments.
Closer to the city centre, the strikes damaged a frequently targeted metro station and destroyed an underground pedestrian passage nearby, leaving a pile of rubble.
Kyiv residents react to Russian strikes
Residents in Kyiv have spoken of their determination and fear after Russia launched an overnight onslaught of strikes on the capital.
A resident who identified himself as Vlad told Reuters he had been inside his apartment when a blast tore off his balcony door, which smashed him in the head.
"My grandmother lives with me, and she can't walk. How could I run away and leave her behind?" he said.
Others continued ordinary life with quiet resilience. Later on Sunday, workers cleared rubble from around the damaged Kyiv metro station and caved-in underpass as passersby looked on, seemingly unfazed.
Fruit vendors resumed business as usual in their makeshift stalls as an excavator poured debris into a dump truck in the background.
"Kyiv will flourish," said Iryna Lomeyko, 68, hinting at the stubborn normality of a neighbourhood that has been heavily damaged by repeated Russian strikes.
"Those idiots destroy everything, but we'll repair it and build anew," she added. "Everything will be alright."
Watch: Russia pounds Kyiv with ballistic missiles leaving one dead and 13 injured
Death toll from Russian strikes rises to six
The number of people killed after a Russian attack on Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities has risen to six, authorities said.
Dozens of others were wounded after Russia launched 41 missiles and 125 drones across Ukraine overnight, according to the Ukrainian air force.
President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said most of the missiles had targeted the capital.
Later on Sunday, a Russian attack on a postal facility near Ukraine's second-largest city of Kharkiv killed three people and wounded at least 20, according to the regional administration.
Russian guided bombs also hit the cities of Kherson and Sumy, local authorities and Ukraine's emergency service reported, with each of the strikes leaving at least one person dead.
Recap: Russian online retail warehouses hit by Ukraine drones in deadly attack
Waves of Ukrainian drone attacks hit Russian oil depots and businesses - including two warehouses for a key online retailer - on Saturday, killing at least nine people.
Kyiv's forces are continuing their relentless aerial campaign against energy infrastructure and military targets inside Russia, aiming to undermine Moscow's war effort and make Russians feel the consequences of the Kremlin's all-out invasion of Ukraine that is well into its fifth year.
Two sprawling facilities used by major Russian retailer, Wildberries, were hit: one in the town of Kotovsk in the Tambov region, roughly 220 miles from the border with Ukraine, and another one in the city of Elektrostal, about some 30 miles east of Moscow.
A Ukrainian drone also hit an oil depot in the city of Noginsk, just north of Elektrostal, sparking a fire and prompting evacuations of a nearby maternity hospital and a residential building.
You can read the full report below:
In pictures: Kyiv surveys damage from overnight Russian onslaught
Zelensky calls for 'acceleration' from European partners over air defence
President Volodymyr Zelensky has called for an "acceleration" from Ukraine's European partners in providing air defence systems following a major aerial assault from Russia overnight.
In a post on X, the Ukrainian president said it is "very important" that both the US and EU understand "that air defence missiles are needed to protect lives literally every single day".
The provision of US-made Patriot air defence systems has becoming a growing issue in the war as Zelensky continues to appeal to the US for stocks to be directed to Ukraine.
new_text:
Ukraine has hit Moscow and the surrounding region with 400 drones overnight, Russian officials said on Monday.
At least two people are reportedly wounded and several buildings were set ablaze in the strikes.
"Overnight, air defence forces and electronic warfare systems repelled a drone attack on Moscow region. The main consequences were recorded in Podolsk, Domodedovo, and the Odintsovo district," Andrei Vorobyov, the governor of the Moscow region, wrote on the Telegram messaging app.
The attack took place between 8:30pm (5.30pm GMT) and 5am (2am GMT).
It follows Ukrainian attacks on three oil depots and a fuel facility in Russia's southern Stavropol region this morning, confirmed by President Volodymyr Zelensky this morning. He said three Russian "shadow fleet" tankers were also hit in the Black Sea.
It comes after a major Russian aerial assault on Kyiv and other cities on Sunday, which Ukrainian authorities said killed at least six people and injured 16 others.
The attack on Ukraine's capital is believed to be the biggest ballistic missile attack of the war.
Russian strikes kill 28 people in Ukraine's Odesa region in July, governor says
Russian strikes have killed 28 people in Ukraine's southern Odesa region in July, the regional governor said on Monday.
"If you take since the beginning of July, the Odesa region was under constant daily shelling. There were days when 13-15 air raid alerts had been announced in the region," Oleh Kiper told Ukrainian television.
Russia says it hit Odesa port fuel reservoirs overnight, Ifax says
Russia's Defence Ministry said on Monday that its forces hit fuel reservoirs at Ukraine's Odesa port overnight, Interfax reported.
Ukraine hits Russian logistics facilities, oil depot in Moscow region, Zelensky says
Ukraine has struck Russian logistics facilities and an oil depot in the Moscow region more than 400 km (250 miles) from Ukrainian border, President Volodymyr Zelensky said on Monday.
"We are responding, justly, to every Russian strike against our cities and communities," Zelensky said in a post on X.
Death toll from Russian strike on vessel near Ukraine's Odesa rises to 10
The death toll from a Russian strike on a vessel near Ukraine's southern port city of Odesa has risen to 10, Ukraine's seaports authority said on Monday.
The Guinea-Bissau-flagged ship loaded with corn carrying a crew from India and Syria was struck late on Sunday, with Ukrainian officials reporting that eight crew members were rescued from the vessel.
Russia says Ukraine launched 400 drones at Moscow region, reports two wounded
Ukraine targeted Moscow and the surrounding region with 400 drones overnight, wounding two people and setting buildings ablaze, Russian officials said this morning.
"Overnight, air defence forces and electronic warfare systems repelled a drone attack on Moscow region. The main consequences were recorded in Podolsk, Domodedovo, and the Odintsovo district," Andrei Vorobyov, the governor of the Moscow region, wrote on the Telegram messaging app.
Vorobyov said two people were wounded and several civilian infrastructure facilities were set ablaze and damaged.
Moscow mayor Sergei Sobyanin said more than 400 "enemy drones" were launched at the Moscow region between 8.30pm (1730 GMT) and 5am (0200 GMT).
Most were neutralised by air defence systems at a long range, including 85 destroyed while approaching the Russian capital.
Wildberries, Russia's largest online retailer, said on Monday its logistics centre in the Podolsk district had been evacuated as a precaution but later resumed normal operations.
The Russian defence ministry said air defence systems had shot down 381 Ukrainian drones over Russian regions and the Black Sea and the Sea of Azov overnight.
Russia fired more ballistic missiles against Ukraine in July than it produces monthly, says ISW
Russian forces have launched more ballistic missiles against Ukraine thus far in July than Russia reportedly produces each month, according to a US-based think tank monitoring the war.
"Ukrainian defence source Militarnyi reported on 19 July that Russia used up to two-thirds of its planned annual production of Zirkon missiles in July 2026 alone and that Russia launched more ballistic missiles in July than Russia's reported total monthly production volume," the Institute for the Study of War said.
Citing the Ukrainian Air Force reports for July, ISW said the attacks indicate that Russia has launched 107 ballistic missiles and 20 Zirkon missiles against Ukraine in July thus far.
"Militarny cited unspecified Ukrainian military intelligence (GUR) reports indicating that Russia produces about 60 Iskander-M ballistic missiles and 40 missiles for the S-400 complex monthly. Russia appears to be dipping into its stockpiles to continue increasing the number of ballistic missiles it launches against Ukraine, as these missiles have a higher success rate than drones and cruise missiles," it said.
"Russian forces have simultaneously decreased the number of drones they launch in each overnight package, launching 2,955 thus far in July 2026 compared to 5,749 in June 2026," the ISW said.
Ukraine blames Russia for strike on Turkish cargo ship that killed 10
Ukrainian officials said a strike by Russian forces targeted an international cargo vessel in the Black Sea off the coast of Ukraine's southern city of Odesa, killing at least 10 crew members.
The Guinea-Bissau-flagged ship had left port loaded with corn and was carrying a crew of 17 sailors from India and Syria, in addition to one Ukrainian, said prime minister Sergii Koretskyi.
Eight crew members were rescued from the vessel - which was still burning late on Sunday - and five remain missing, he added.
"A civilian ship. An international crew. A route serving global food security. Putin saw another target," Ukraine's foreign minister Andrii Sybiha wrote on X.
"This attack leaves no room for illusions. Putin's Russia does not care whom it kills," he said.
A Reuters witness in Odesa saw smoke billowing from a large vessel off the coast.
Moscow has not publicly commented on Sunday's strike.
Russia has stepped up attacks in recent weeks on deepwater ports in southern Ukraine that handle much of the country's grain and other critical cargo in the fifth year of war.
Death toll from Russian strike on vessel near Odesa rises to 10
The death toll from a Russian strike on a vessel near Ukraine's southern port city of Odesa has risen to 10, Ukraine's seaports authority said this morning.
The Guinea-Bissau-flagged ship loaded with corn carrying a crew from India and Syria was struck late on Sunday, with Ukrainian officials reporting that eight crew members were rescued from the vessel.
New video shows Monaco parcel bomb blast attack on Ukrainian tycoon
Fresh footage has been released of the moment a Ukrainian property tycoon and two others were injured in a bomb attack in Monaco.
Tycoon Vadym Iermolaiev was left fighting for life after the explosion at the start of June, with a 13 year-old boy and a woman also injured in what authorities believe was an attempted assassination.
Ukraine's prosecutor general Ruslan Kravchenko released footage of the incident as he reported an investigation was underway to obtain new evidence in the case of "attempted murder".
The video showed a hooded figure appearing to drop a backpack full of explosives on the steps of Mr Iermolaiev's Monaco home. As they walked away, the property tycoon could be seen walking towards the property with two others when a purple flash erupted as the explosion took place.
